<content>
1: ---
2: title: "System Code Graph Naming Conventions"
3: description: "Reference for the deliberate name asymmetry across skill folder, MCP server, launcher, database directory, plugin bridge, and hook location."
4: trigger_phrases:
5:   - "system code graph naming"
6:   - "mk-code-index vs system-code-graph"
7:   - "mk_code_index naming"
8:   - "code graph plugin name"
9:   - "code graph hook location"
10: importance_tier: "important"
11: contextType: "general"
12: ---
13: 
14: # System Code Graph Naming Conventions
15: 
16: A reference for the multiple names that refer to this skill across different runtime layers and why each one is intentional.
17: 
18: ---
19: 
20: ## 1. OVERVIEW
21: 
22: ### Purpose
23: 
24: The `system-code-graph` skill is referenced under five different identifiers depending on which layer of the system is calling it. Each name is correct in its own scope, but the asymmetry surprises operators and led to documentation drift in earlier packets. This reference is the single source of truth.
25: 
26: ### When to Use
27: 
28: - Explaining `system-code-graph` vs `mk-code-index` vs `mk_code_index`.
29: - Updating runtime configs, launcher names, plugin bridges, or docs.
30: - Avoiding accidental renames that break saved MCP tool IDs.
31: 
32: ### Core Principle
33: 
34: Names are stable per layer; do not normalize them across layers unless a packet explicitly migrates every caller.
35: 
36: ### Key Sources
37: 
38: - ADR-001 (`005-code-graph/013-system-code-graph-extraction/`): MCP server name stability.
39: - ADR-002 (`005-code-graph/014-design-and-decision-record/`): Skill folder slug + database path policy.
40: - Cross-runtime consolidation packet (`005-code-graph/019-system-code-graph-uplift/`): launcher and shared-data-dir rename rationale.
41: 
42: ---
43: 
44: ## 2. NAME MAP
45: 
46: | Layer | Identifier | Source-of-truth | Reason |
47: |---|---|---|---|
48: | Skill folder slug | `system-code-graph` | `.opencode/skills/system-code-graph/` | Filesystem name; matches `name:` field in SKILL.md frontmatter. |
49: | MCP server name | `mk-code-index` | `opencode.json` `mcp.mk_code_index.*` and 5 runtime mirrors | Stable tool contract per ADR-002. Renaming would break every config that registers the server. |
50: | MCP server config key | `mk_code_index` | All runtime configs | MCP convention: hyphens in server names become underscores in config keys. |
51: | MCP tool namespace prefix | `mcp__mk_code_index__` | Tool call sites | MCP convention: hyphens → underscores in tool prefix. |
52: | Launcher file | `mk-code-index-launcher.cjs` | `.opencode/bin/mk-code-index-launcher.cjs` | Filesystem path uses the hyphenated server identity. |
53: | Database directory | `.opencode/skills/system-code-graph/mcp_server/database/` | `INSTALL_GUIDE.md §7` | Skill-local data dir; folder name uses `code-graph` (skill-domain) to keep the path readable across runtimes. |
54: | Plugin bridge file | `mk-code-graph-bridge.mjs` | `.opencode/skills/system-code-graph/mcp_server/plugin_bridges/mk-code-graph-bridge.mjs` (spawned by the OpenCode plugin `.opencode/plugins/mk-code-graph.js`) | Matches the `code-graph` domain word and the symmetry pattern used by `system-skill-advisor` (whose plugin bridge is `mk-skill-advisor-bridge.mjs`). The bridge routes through the daemon-backed CLI shim in warm-only mode. |
55: | CLI shim | `code-index.cjs` | `.opencode/bin/code-index.cjs` | Daemon-backed CLI uses the hyphenated server identity without the `mk-` prefix; exposes the same tool ids as the MCP namespace as an additive dual-stack fallback (`--warm-only`, exit `75` = retryable). |
56: | Hook source location | `.opencode/skills/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/hooks/` | Hook source tree | Asymmetric vs the skill-owned hook pattern. See §4 below. |
57: 
58: ---
59: 
60: ## 3. MCP SERVER NAME STABILITY (`mk-code-index` vs `system-code-graph`)
61: 
62: The MCP server name `mk-code-index` is a stable tool contract. Renaming the server to match the skill folder slug (`system-code-graph`) would invalidate every config entry across all four runtimes (OpenCode, Claude Code, Codex, VSCode) and would break every saved tool call ID (`mcp__mk_code_index__*`) in agent transcripts, memory records, and dispatched task logs.
63: 
64: ADR-002 settled this: the **skill folder slug** describes what the skill is for; the **MCP server name** is the runtime identity contract; the two are allowed to diverge and the project tolerates the asymmetry in exchange for tool-call stability.
65: 
66: ### What this means in practice
67: 
68: - New docs that talk about the skill as a concept use `system-code-graph`.
69: - New docs that talk about MCP invocation use `mk-code-index` (server name) or `mk_code_index` (config key).
70: - Tool call IDs always start with `mcp__mk_code_index__`.
71: 
72: ---
73: 
74: ## 4. HOOK LOCATION ASYMMETRY (vs skill-advisor)
75: 
76: SessionStart hooks for code-graph (`session-prime.ts`, `session-start.ts`, and their downstream helpers) live under `.opencode/skills/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/hooks/`, NOT under `.opencode/skills/system-code-graph/hooks/`. This is an intentional asymmetry vs the `system-skill-advisor` pattern where hooks are skill-owned (ADR-001 for skill-advisor).
77: 
78: ### Why hooks stayed in spec-kit
79: 
80: - The hook path is referenced by **110+ files** across the repo, including `.claude/settings.local.json`, runtime config bootstrap paths, build config dependencies, and other skill manifests.
81: - Migrating the path would be a high-risk breaking change with no immediate operational benefit. The hooks reach code-graph data through the stable boundary at `system-spec-kit/mcp_server/lib/code-graph-boundary.ts`.
82: - A future migration would need its own packet with build and config redesign scope; it is currently deferred.
83: 
84: ### Implication for code-graph maintainers
85: 
86: When editing SessionStart-related code that needs to read or write code-graph state, look in `system-spec-kit/mcp_server/hooks/` and `system-spec-kit/mcp_server/lib/code-graph-boundary.ts`, not `system-code-graph/mcp_server/`.
87: 
88: ---
89: 
90: ## 5. DATABASE LOCATION
91: 
92: The code-graph SQLite triplet (`code-graph.sqlite`, `.sqlite-wal`, `.sqlite-shm`), readiness marker (`.code-graph-readiness.json`), and launcher state (`.mk-code-index-launcher.json`) all live in the skill-local data directory:
93: 
94: ```text
95: .opencode/skills/system-code-graph/mcp_server/database/
96: ```
97: 
98: This location is SKILL-LOCAL. The database was briefly consolidated to a shared workspace-root location (`.opencode/.spec-kit/code-graph/`), but that was reversed on 2026-05-29 — every runtime already shares the skill through the `.opencode/skills` symlink, so the skill-local path is already a single shared instance and the separate shared location added no coordination benefit. See [`../config/database_path_policy.md`](../config/database_path_policy.md) for the full migration record and override rules.
99: 
100: ---
101: 
102: ## 6. RELATED RESOURCES
103: 
104: - [`../config/database_path_policy.md`](../config/database_path_policy.md) — full policy + override rules for the database path.
105: - [`ownership_boundary.md`](ownership_boundary.md) — what stays in `system-spec-kit` vs `system-code-graph` after extraction.
106: - [`../readiness/code_graph_readiness_check.md`](../readiness/code_graph_readiness_check.md) — readiness contract that the launcher and read-path tools enforce.
107: - [`../../INSTALL_GUIDE.md`](../../INSTALL_GUIDE.md) — canonical configuration and verification steps for `mk_code_index` setup.

(End of file - total 107 lines)
</content>

# System Code Graph Naming Conventions

A reference for the multiple names that refer to this skill across different runtime layers and why each one is intentional.

---

## 1. OVERVIEW

### Purpose

The `system-code-graph` skill is referenced under five different identifiers depending on which layer of the system is calling it. Each name is correct in its own scope, but the asymmetry surprises operators and led to documentation drift in earlier packets. This reference is the single source of truth.

### When to Use

- Explaining `system-code-graph` vs `mk-code-index` vs `mk_code_index`.
- Updating runtime configs, launcher names, plugin bridges, or docs.
- Avoiding accidental renames that break saved MCP tool IDs.

### Core Principle

Names are stable per layer; do not normalize them across layers unless a packet explicitly migrates every caller.

### Key Sources

- ADR-001 (`005-code-graph/013-system-code-graph-extraction/`): MCP server name stability.
- ADR-002 (`005-code-graph/014-design-and-decision-record/`): Skill folder slug + database path policy.
- Cross-runtime consolidation packet (`005-code-graph/019-system-code-graph-uplift/`): launcher and shared-data-dir rename rationale.

---

## 2. NAME MAP

| Layer | Identifier | Source-of-truth | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| Skill folder slug | `system-code-graph` | `.opencode/skills/system-code-graph/` | Filesystem name; matches `name:` field in SKILL.md frontmatter. |
| MCP server name | `mk-code-index` | `opencode.json` `mcp.mk_code_index.*` and 5 runtime mirrors | Stable tool contract per ADR-002. Renaming would break every config that registers the server. |
| MCP server config key | `mk_code_index` | All runtime configs | MCP convention: hyphens in server names become underscores in config keys. |
| MCP tool namespace prefix | `mcp__mk_code_index__` | Tool call sites | MCP convention: hyphens → underscores in tool prefix. |
| Launcher file | `mk-code-index-launcher.cjs` | `.opencode/bin/mk-code-index-launcher.cjs` | Filesystem path uses the hyphenated server identity. |
| Database directory | `.opencode/skills/system-code-graph/mcp_server/database/` | `INSTALL_GUIDE.md §7` | Skill-local data dir; folder name uses `code-graph` (skill-domain) to keep the path readable across runtimes. |
| Plugin bridge file | `mk-code-graph-bridge.mjs` | `.opencode/skills/system-code-graph/mcp_server/plugin_bridges/mk-code-graph-bridge.mjs` (spawned by the OpenCode plugin `.opencode/plugins/mk-code-graph.js`) | Matches the `code-graph` domain word and the symmetry pattern used by `system-skill-advisor` (whose plugin bridge is `mk-skill-advisor-bridge.mjs`). The bridge routes through the daemon-backed CLI shim in warm-only mode. |
| CLI shim | `code-index.cjs` | `.opencode/bin/code-index.cjs` | Daemon-backed CLI uses the hyphenated server identity without the `mk-` prefix; exposes the same tool ids as the MCP namespace as an additive dual-stack fallback (`--warm-only`, exit `75` = retryable). |
| Hook source location | `.opencode/skills/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/hooks/` | Hook source tree | Asymmetric vs the skill-owned hook pattern. See §4 below. |

---

## 3. MCP SERVER NAME STABILITY (`mk-code-index` vs `system-code-graph`)

The MCP server name `mk-code-index` is a stable tool contract. Renaming the server to match the skill folder slug (`system-code-graph`) would invalidate every config entry across all four runtimes (OpenCode, Claude Code, Codex, VSCode) and would break every saved tool call ID (`mcp__mk_code_index__*`) in agent transcripts, memory records, and dispatched task logs.

ADR-002 settled this: the **skill folder slug** describes what the skill is for; the **MCP server name** is the runtime identity contract; the two are allowed to diverge and the project tolerates the asymmetry in exchange for tool-call stability.

### What this means in practice

- New docs that talk about the skill as a concept use `system-code-graph`.
- New docs that talk about MCP invocation use `mk-code-index` (server name) or `mk_code_index` (config key).
- Tool call IDs always start with `mcp__mk_code_index__`.

---

## 4. HOOK LOCATION ASYMMETRY (vs skill-advisor)

SessionStart hooks for code-graph (`session-prime.ts`, `session-start.ts`, and their downstream helpers) live under `.opencode/skills/system-spec-kit/mcp_server/hooks/`, NOT under `.opencode/skills/system-code-graph/hooks/`. This is an intentional asymmetry vs the `system-skill-advisor` pattern where hooks are skill-owned (ADR-001 for skill-advisor).

### Why hooks stayed in spec-kit

- The hook path is referenced by **110+ files** across the repo, including `.claude/settings.local.json`, runtime config bootstrap paths, build config dependencies, and other skill manifests.
- Migrating the path would be a high-risk breaking change with no immediate operational benefit. The hooks reach code-graph data through the stable boundary at `system-spec-kit/mcp_server/lib/code-graph-boundary.ts`.
- A future migration would need its own packet with build and config redesign scope; it is currently deferred.

### Implication for code-graph maintainers

When editing SessionStart-related code that needs to read or write code-graph state, look in `system-spec-kit/mcp_server/hooks/` and `system-spec-kit/mcp_server/lib/code-graph-boundary.ts`, not `system-code-graph/mcp_server/`.

---

## 5. DATABASE LOCATION

The code-graph SQLite triplet (`code-graph.sqlite`, `.sqlite-wal`, `.sqlite-shm`), readiness marker (`.code-graph-readiness.json`), and launcher state (`.mk-code-index-launcher.json`) all live in the skill-local data directory:

```text
.opencode/skills/system-code-graph/mcp_server/database/
```

This location is SKILL-LOCAL. The database was briefly consolidated to a shared workspace-root location (`.opencode/.spec-kit/code-graph/`), but that was reversed on 2026-05-29 — every runtime already shares the skill through the `.opencode/skills` symlink, so the skill-local path is already a single shared instance and the separate shared location added no coordination benefit. See [`../config/database_path_policy.md`](../config/database_path_policy.md) for the full migration record and override rules.

---

## 6. RELATED RESOURCES

- [`../config/database_path_policy.md`](../config/database_path_policy.md) — full policy + override rules for the database path.
- [`ownership_boundary.md`](ownership_boundary.md) — what stays in `system-spec-kit` vs `system-code-graph` after extraction.
- [`../readiness/code_graph_readiness_check.md`](../readiness/code_graph_readiness_check.md) — readiness contract that the launcher and read-path tools enforce.
- [`../../INSTALL_GUIDE.md`](../../INSTALL_GUIDE.md) — canonical configuration and verification steps for `mk_code_index` setup.

1: #!/usr/bin/env node

3: 
4: import { spawn } from 'node:child_process';
5: import { existsSync } from 'node:fs';
6: import { createRequire } from 'node:module';

8: 
9: const SERVICE_NAME = 'mk-code-index';
10: const DEFAULT_SOCKET_DIR = '/tmp/mk-code-index';
11: const DEFAULT_TIMEOUT_MS = 2500;
12: const DEFAULT_PROBE_TIMEOUT_MS = 100;
13: const MAX_STDIO_BYTES = 1024 * 1024;
14: const CLI_SHIM = fileURLToPath(new URL('../../../../bin/code-index.cjs', import.meta.url));
15: const BRIDGE_PATH = fileURLToPath(new URL('../../../../bin/lib/launcher-ipc-bridge.cjs', import.meta.url));
16: const DB_DIR = fileURLToPath(new URL('../database', import.meta.url));

18: // Normalized form folds snake/kebab/camel into one key so the maintenance blocklist
19: // cannot be bypassed by passing --tool codeGraphScan instead of code_graph_scan; the
20: // downstream CLI resolves all three alias casings to the same canonical maintenance tool.
21: function normalizeToolName(name) {
22:   return String(name).replace(/[-_]/g, '').toLowerCase();
23: }
24: 
25: const MAINTENANCE_TOOLS = new Set([
26:   'code_graph_scan',
27:   'code_graph_apply',
28:   'code_graph_verify',
29: ].map(normalizeToolName));
30: 
31: function response(args) {
32:   return {
33:     status: ['ok', 'skipped', 'fail_open'].includes(args.status) ? args.status : 'fail_open',
34:     brief: typeof args.brief === 'string' && args.brief.trim() ? args.brief : null,
35:     data: args.data ?? null,
36:     metadata: args.metadata && typeof args.metadata === 'object' ? args.metadata : {},
37:     ...(args.error ? { error: String(args.error).toUpperCase().replace(/[^A-Z0-9_]/g, '_') || 'UNKNOWN' } : {}),
38:   };
39: }
40: 
41: function failOpen(error, metadata = {}) {
42:   return response({ status: 'fail_open', brief: null, data: null, error, metadata });
43: }
44: 
45: function skipped(error, metadata = {}) {
46:   return response({ status: 'skipped', brief: null, data: null, error, metadata });
47: }
48: 
49: function positiveInt(value, fallback) {
50:   return Number.isFinite(value) && value > 0 ? Math.trunc(value) : fallback;
51: }
52: 
53: function isRecord(value) {
54:   return value && typeof value === 'object' && !Array.isArray(value);
55: }
56: 
57: function unwrapData(payload) {
58:   return isRecord(payload) && Object.prototype.hasOwnProperty.call(payload, 'data')
59:     ? payload.data
60:     : payload;
61: }
62: 
63: function stringField(record, field) {
64:   if (!isRecord(record)) return null;
65:   const value = record[field];
66:   return typeof value === 'string' && value.trim() ? value.trim() : null;
67: }
68: 
69: function scalarField(record, field) {
70:   if (!isRecord(record)) return null;
71:   const value = record[field];
72:   if (typeof value === 'string' && value.trim()) return value.trim();
73:   if (typeof value === 'number' || typeof value === 'boolean') return String(value);
74:   return null;
75: }
76: 
77: function recordField(record, field) {
78:   if (!isRecord(record)) return null;
79:   const value = record[field];
80:   return isRecord(value) ? value : null;
81: }
82: 
83: function capOutput(current, chunk) {
84:   if (current.length >= MAX_STDIO_BYTES) return current;
85:   return (current + String(chunk)).slice(0, MAX_STDIO_BYTES);
86: }
87: 
88: function parseStdout(stdout) {
89:   const trimmed = stdout.trim();
90:   if (!trimmed) return null;
91:   try {
92:     return JSON.parse(trimmed);
93:   } catch {
94:     return null;
95:   }
96: }
97: 
98: function classifyCliFailure(exitCode, stderr, timedOut) {
99:   const staleDist = exitCode === 69 && /dist entrypoint is (stale|missing)/i.test(stderr);
100:   if (staleDist) {
101:     return {
102:       status: 'fail_open',
103:       brief: 'dist stale, rebuild required',
104:       error: 'dist_stale_rebuild_required',
105:       retryable: false,

108:         staleDist: true,
109:         rebuildRequired: true,
110:         action: 'run tsc -p .opencode/skills/system-code-graph/tsconfig.json',
111:       },
112:     };
113:   }
114:   const retryable = exitCode === 75 || timedOut;
115:   return {
116:     status: retryable ? 'skipped' : 'fail_open',
117:     brief: null,
118:     error: timedOut ? 'timeout' : `exit_${exitCode ?? 'unknown'}`,
119:     retryable,
120:     metadata: {},
121:   };
122: }
123: 
124: function withDefaultSocketEnv(fn) {
125:   const previous = process.env.SPECKIT_IPC_SOCKET_DIR;
126:   if (!previous) {
127:     process.env.SPECKIT_IPC_SOCKET_DIR = DEFAULT_SOCKET_DIR;
128:   }
129:   try {
130:     return fn();
131:   } finally {
132:     if (!previous) {
133:       delete process.env.SPECKIT_IPC_SOCKET_DIR;
134:     }
135:   }
136: }
137: 
138: async function warmProbe(timeoutMs) {
139:   if (!existsSync(CLI_SHIM) || !existsSync(BRIDGE_PATH)) {
140:     return { warm: false, reason: 'missing_runtime_assets' };
141:   }
142:   const require = createRequire(import.meta.url);
143:   const bridge = require(BRIDGE_PATH);
144:   return withDefaultSocketEnv(async () => {
145:     const socketPath = bridge.getIpcSocketPath(SERVICE_NAME, { dbDir: DB_DIR });
146:     if (!socketPath.startsWith('tcp://') && !existsSync(socketPath)) {
147:       return { warm: false, reason: 'socket_absent', socketPath };
148:     }
149:     if (process.platform === 'darwin' && !socketPath.startsWith('tcp://') && Buffer.byteLength(socketPath) > 103) {
150:       return { warm: false, reason: 'socket_path_too_long', socketPath };
151:     }
152:     const probe = await bridge.probeDaemon(socketPath, {
153:       timeoutMs: Math.max(1, timeoutMs),
154:       deepProbe: true,
155:     });
156:     return probe.status === 'alive'
157:       ? { warm: true, reason: probe.reason ?? 'alive', socketPath }
158:       : { warm: false, reason: probe.reason ?? probe.status, socketPath };
159:   });
160: }
161: 
162: function renderCodeGraphBrief(payload, maxChars = 2400) {
163:   const data = unwrapData(payload);
164:   const root = isRecord(payload) ? payload : null;
165:   const readiness = recordField(data, 'readiness');
166:   const fallback = recordField(data, 'fallbackDecision');
167:   const status = root ? stringField(root, 'status') ?? 'unknown' : 'unknown';
168:   const message = root ? stringField(root, 'message') ?? stringField(root, 'error') : null;
169:   const parts = [

13: // When ambiguous between production code and a test
14: // fixture with the same name, prefer the production candidate. Test fixtures
15: // frequently reuse production symbol names (e.g. `getDefaultConfig` defined
16: // inside a vitest spec to exercise the resolver against itself), and treating
17: // those as equally-weighted candidates makes the resolver conservatively skip
18: // edges that should have resolved cleanly to the production target.
19: const TEST_FILE_PATTERN = /\.(?:test|vitest|spec)(?:-d)?\.[a-z]+$/;

1: // ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
2: // MODULE: Code Graph Config Defaults
3: // ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
4: // Single source of truth for hardcoded config values.
5: // All scalar defaults derive from env-var overrides; object defaults
6: // accept JSON overrides via env var, merging partial overrides the way
7: // the skill-advisor env-override path does.
8: 
9: function parsePositiveInt(value: string | undefined, fallback: number): number {
10:   if (value === undefined) return fallback;
11:   const parsed = Number.parseInt(value, 10);
12:   if (Number.isNaN(parsed) || parsed <= 0) return fallback;
13:   return parsed;
14: }
15: 
16: function parseJsonOverride<T extends Record<string, unknown>>(
17:   value: string | undefined,
18:   fallback: T,
19: ): T {
20:   if (value === undefined) return fallback;
21:   try {
22:     const parsed = JSON.parse(value) as Partial<T>;
23:     return { ...fallback, ...parsed };
24:   } catch (err) {
25:     console.warn(
26:       `[config-defaults] Failed to parse JSON env var, using default: ${err instanceof Error ? err.message : String(err)}`,
27:     );
28:     return fallback;
29:   }
30: }

1: // ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
2: // MODULE: Code Graph Auto-Rescan Policy
3: // ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
4: // Shared policy module that decides whether the read-path
5: // handlers (query, context) may safely auto-trigger a guarded
6: // inline full scan when ensure-ready returns
7: // `action: "full_scan"`.
8: //
9: // A scope-fingerprint guard in scan.ts prevents unsafe scope-mismatched scans
10: // from wiping the
11: // populated index. With that guard in place, it is now SAFE to
12: // allow read-path handlers to auto-rescan when stored scope
13: // matches the active runtime scope AND the parse-diagnostics
14: // backlog is clean.
15: //
16: // Caller contract:
17: //   - If `shouldAutoRescan(...)` returns true, the caller is
18: //     authorized to run an inline full scan.
19: //   - If it returns false, the caller MUST NOT run a full scan
20: //     and MUST surface the diagnostics payload so
21: //     operators can choose remediation.
22: //
23: // Note: ensure-ready.ts maintains its own internal copy of this
24: // gate via `evaluateGuardedFullScan()` so the diagnostics on
25: // `ReadyResult.autoRescanSafety` / `autoRescanBlockReason`
